Project ASTRA
Mission Design Prototype Network Roadmap
1420 MHz • Hydrogen Line • Student Built

Radio astronomy for every curious student.

Project ASTRA is a low-cost, customizable radio telescope platform for schools, astronomy CCAs, and research students — built to make hydrogen-line astronomy, RF signal processing, telescope engineering, and interferometry accessible.

Explore the Mission View Prototype
1 mASTRA 100A
0.4f/D ratio
21 cmHI line
Mission

Make the invisible sky accessible.

ASTRA exists to turn radio astronomy from something locked inside expensive facilities into a hands-on learning platform that students can build, modify, observe with, and expand into collaborative telescope arrays.

Problem & Context

Students need access to real signals, not just theory.

Professional radio telescopes are expensive, difficult to access, and usually not designed for student-level customization. ASTRA bridges the gap by combining an affordable dish, feedhorn, receiver chain, and software pipeline that students can understand from first principles.

01

Astrophysics

Observe the 1420 MHz neutral hydrogen line to explore the structure and dynamics of the Milky Way.

02

Engineering

Build a parabolic reflector, feedhorn, Az-El mount, gearing system, and stable mechanical platform.

03

Computing

Process spectra through SDR and software tools, then extend toward synchronization and correlation.

System Design

A modular 1420 MHz telescope platform.

The ASTRA 100A uses a 1-meter parabolic reflector, pyramidal feedhorn, Az-El rotating base, low-noise amplifier, software-defined radio, and computer-side signal processing.

ASTRA 100A

Designed for real student construction.

The reflector is split into printable segments, then sanded and covered with a conductive aluminium layer to reflect incoming radio waves toward the focus. The structure supports both quick assembly and more permanent screw or rivet-based construction.

  • 1-meter parabolic dish with f/D = 0.4
  • 3D-printed segmented reflector
  • Aluminium reflective surface
  • Portable design for school deployment

Pyramidal feedhorn

Selected for ease of fabrication, controllable beamwidth, and practical 1420 MHz dish illumination.

Az-El mount

Points the telescope across the sky using azimuth and elevation movement with stepper motor control.

Receiver chain

Signal path: feedhorn probe → low-noise amplifier → software-defined radio → processing software.

Hardware Pipeline

From cosmic radio waves to student data.

Step 01

Telescope

The dish and feedhorn collect and couple weak 1420 MHz radio signals.

Step 02

LNA

A low-noise amplifier placed near the feed preserves the faint signal.

Step 03

SDR

A software-defined radio digitizes the received spectrum.

Step 04

Software

GNU Radio or custom tools process, visualize, and store observations.

Models

ASTRA 100A and ASTRA 230A.

ASTRA begins with a feasible 1-meter prototype and extends toward a larger 2.3-meter design for improved capability and comparison with commercial systems.

SpecificationASTRA 100AASTRA 230A
Dish diameter1 meter2.3 meters
Focal ratio0.40.4
Reflector typeParabolicParabolic
FeedPyramidal feedhornPyramidal feedhorn
MountAz-El rotating baseLarger Az-El rotating base
Estimated weight~4.056 kg≤ 28 kg target
Prototype Gallery

From CAD to physical build.

ASTRA is built around practical fabrication: printed dish sections, mechanical ribs, secure clips, reflective lining, and a receiver chain that students can assemble and understand.

Dish structureSegmented reflector with ribs and panel joints.
Dish geometryCAD view of reflector shape and focal structure.
Assembly detailsPrinted sections designed for repeatable construction.
Reflector panelsPanel alignment and surface finishing process.
FasteningClips and screws improve rigidity and portability.
Long-Term Vision

An island-wide student interferometry network.

The ideal future is a modular network of school-based ASTRA telescopes. Each school can use its telescope independently, while the wider network can explore synchronization, correlation, and interferometric concepts.

Accessible Collaboration

One telescope becomes many.

The project’s second phase explores synchronization between telescopes. A close-distance proof of concept can use a shared master clock, while longer baselines may require GPS-disciplined oscillators.

  • School nodes connected through an online network
  • Research into phase comparison and correlation
  • Collaborative STEM platform for students
Roadmap

Project timeline.

January
Ideation and research.
February
3D modelling and design.
March
Fabrication and early prototype work.
April
Proposal evaluation and design refinement.
May
Complete core ASTRA 100A build.
June–July
ASTRA 230A planning, synchronization blueprint, and proof-of-concept testing.
August
Final evaluation, documentation, and public project presentation.
